message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 31
Recent Green phase: No code change — "two swords; two sword damage entries → each own deductible" passed immediately, confirming each damage entry is processed independently with its own 100 deductible.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. No code changed this cycle; document if nothing is warranted.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 31 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the implementation for refactoring opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (First Priority)

The Green phase added no code — the test "two sword damage entries → each own deductible" passed immediately because `processClaim` already iterates `step.incident.damages` and calls `damagePayout` per entry, each applying its own deductible. So this cycle introduced no new naming that needs evaluation.

Reviewing existing names against current intent:
- `damagePayout(damage, items)` — clear, returns the payout for a single damage after reimbursement and deductible.
- `processClaim` — clear.
- `lookupByType`, `unitBase`, `baseForType`, `componentBase` — all reveal intent.

No name misrepresents its function. Naming is fine.

## Step 2: Inspect for refactoring opportunities

Let me look closely at one spot. In `damagePayout` (line 148-151), the variable name `item` is used for `items.find(...)`. The found item is the insured item matching the damage's type. This reads well.

Let me check `processClaim` — there's a subtle point. The `desired` reduction (line 164-167) sums each damage's payout independently, each subtracting `DEDUCTIBLE`. This is exactly what the new test confirmed. The code already expresses "each damage entry gets its own deductible" correctly because `damagePayout` applies `DEDUCTIBLE` per call inside the reduce.

Let me evaluate APP mass and look for a genuine improvement. One candidate: `processClaim` returns both `payout` and `remainingCap`, mutates the map AND returns `remainingCap`. The returned `remainingCap` is used by the result shape; the map mutation tracks across claims. This is necessary state, not redundant.

Let me look for a meaningful improvement candidate in the damage-summing logic — the `desired` reduce could be extracted to a named helper for intent clarity.

## Step 3 & 4: Apply Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — extract the desired-payout sum

The inline `reduce` in `processClaim` mixes the "what would we like to pay across all damages" computation with cap/flooring orchestration. Extracting it names the concept and confirms the per-damage-deductible behavior the new test exercises.

**APP mass of the inline reduce expression** (lines 164-167):
- `desired` binding: 1
- `step.incident.damages` (member access binding chain): counts as binding ~1
- `.reduce` invocation: 2
- `sum` binding: 1, `damage` binding: 1
- `+` invocation: 2
- `damagePayout(...)` invocation: 2
- `policyStep.items` binding: 1
- literal `0`: 1

Extracting to a helper `desiredPayout(damages, items)` keeps total mass essentially equal (a helper adds one invocation, ~2) but improves intent. Per the rules, Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP. Let me make this single, focused change.

content.text:
All 31 tests still pass.

## Refactoring Complete

**Naming Evaluation (first priority):** No new code was added this cycle — the "two sword damage entries → each own deductible" test passed immediately because `processClaim` already iterates `step.incident.damages` and `damagePayout` applies its own `DEDUCTIBLE` per entry. All existing names (`damagePayout`, `processClaim`, `lookupByType`, etc.) accurately reveal intent. No rename warranted.

**Refactoring applied (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):**
Extracted the inline `damages.reduce(...)` inside `processClaim` into a named helper `desiredPayout(damages, items)`. This names the concept the new test exercises — the total desired payout summed across damage entries, each carrying its own deductible — and separates that computation from the cap/flooring orchestration in `processClaim`.

**APP mass:** Essentially unchanged. The reduce expression moved out verbatim; `processClaim` loses the inline reduce (~14 mass) and gains one invocation (`desiredPayout(...)`, +2), while the new helper carries the moved mass. Net total is roughly flat. Per the rules, Rule 2 (clarity) is the driver here, not mass reduction — `processClaim`'s body now reads as a clean sequence: look up remaining cap, compute desired payout, apply cap and floor.

**Rules evaluated:**
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 31 pass before and after.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Improved — extracted and named `desiredPayout`.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): No duplication present.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): One helper added; justified by the clarity gain in `processClaim`.

**File changed:** `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_05-27-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

**Tests:** All passing.
